title: Biometrics survey — iris, keystroke, palmprint, retina, signature, voice
html:
TL;DR

A page from the Jain/Pankanti/Prabhakar IEEE biometrics survey — pulled from ieee xplore via the osu proxy on March 29, 2025, screenshotted by Yahir on April 27. This page walks the modality zoo: iris, keystroke, odor, palmprint, retinal scan, signature, voice — with a one-paragraph rip on each one's accuracy, spoofability, and user-friction profile. Closes with the survey's central honest take: no single biometric wins everywhere; the right one depends on the application domain.

Why it's in the graph

This is the first slice of OSU material to land in the vault. Likely from a security course (CS 372 or CS 419/AI in security, or possibly the senior project — this and the 2025 06 01 mobile malware survey felt page suggest a security/privacy seminar in spring/summer 2025). It's also the academic backstop for any future Yahir thinking about identity, fraud, surveillance, KYC, or AI-vs-deepfake recognition.

The survey is canonical — Jain wrote the textbook of the field. If rankett or any future product touches identity verification, this is the foundational paper to cite.

What's actually on the page

  • iris recognition — distinctive even between identical twins, hard to surgically alter, trivial to detect designer contact lenses. Newer systems are user-friendly and cheap.
  • keystroke dynamics — behavioral, not unique, but discriminative. Can be monitored unobtrusively.
  • Odor — chemical sensors over a "whiff of air"; speculative whether deodorants/environment break it.
  • Palmprint — bigger area than fingerprints = more distinctive, but bulkier scanners. Combine with hand geometry, principal lines, wrinkles → highly accurate.
  • Retinal scan — most secure, least replicable, but invasive (eye-piece contact + active focus) and leaks medical info (hypertension). Public hates it.
  • Signature — already legally accepted; behavioral and shifts with mood/health; pro forgers can defeat it.
  • voice recognition — hybrid physio + behavioral; text-dependent vs text-independent; degraded by phone audio, but the only modality that fits tele-banking.

The summary line: "each biometric technique is admissible and there is no optimal biometric characteristic."

Connections worth chasing

  • The retinal-scan-leaks-hypertension fact is exactly the biometric privacy tradeoff in microcosm — security and privacy aren't opposites here, they're coupled.
  • Voice as the tele-banking-fit modality is interesting given how aggressively LLMs and speech-clone models have moved since this 2003-vintage paper. The whole modality ranking is overdue for a re-rank.
  • Compare with the mobile malware paper Yahir screenshotted six weeks later — this is the defensive side of the same identity/access-control problem.

Wikilinks
